questions.question_text: Colorado follows which water law doctrine?
questions.explanation: Colorado follows the prior appropriation doctrine — 'first in time, first in right.' Water rights are separate from land ownership, unlike riparian rights states.

questions.question_text: Under prior appropriation, a water right holder with an 1890 priority date compared to one with a 1950 date is:
distractors: Junior and waits for the 1950 user
questions.explanation: Under prior appropriation, the earlier priority date makes a right senior. Senior rights holders get their full allocation before junior rights holders receive anything.

questions.question_text: Water rights in Colorado are adjudicated through the:
questions.explanation: Colorado has 7 water divisions with Water Courts corresponding to major river basins. These courts adjudicate water rights disputes and claims.

questions.question_text: A rancher sells their land but retains the water rights. Under Colorado law, this is:
distractors: Not permitted — water goes with the land
questions.explanation: In Colorado, water rights are separate from land ownership and can be bought, sold, and transferred independently of the land.

questions.question_text: A developer wants to use stream water that would reduce flow to a senior rights holder. The developer must obtain:
questions.explanation: Augmentation plans are required when new water use would deplete stream flow affecting senior rights. Junior users must replace water to protect senior rights holders.

questions.question_text: A seller whose property has a private well must disclose:
questions.correct_answer: The well's permit and legal status
distractors: Only the depth of the well
Only the water quality test results
questions.explanation: Sellers must disclose whether the well is a permitted well and its legal status. Change of ownership may also trigger reporting requirements.

questions.question_text: An exempt well in Colorado typically serves:
questions.explanation: Exempt wells (typically domestic) serve fewer than 3 single-family dwellings and have specific limitations on use compared to fully permitted wells.

questions.question_text: The source of water disclosure is required for properties not served by:
questions.explanation: The source of water disclosure is triggered when a property is not served by a municipal water system — covering wells, springs, cisterns, and water districts.

questions.question_text: When a property with a well changes ownership, this may trigger:
distractors: Transfer of water rights to the county
questions.explanation: Change of ownership for a well may trigger reporting requirements to the state. The well itself does not close, and existing permits typically transfer with the property.

questions.question_text: A property uses a spring as its sole water source. The seller must provide:
questions.explanation: Any non-municipal water source — including springs, wells, cisterns, or water districts — requires the seller to provide a source of water disclosure.

questions.question_text: Who determines the value of real property for tax purposes in Colorado?
questions.explanation: The County Assessor determines property value. The mill levy (tax rate) is set by local taxing authorities, and the tax equals assessed value times mill levy.

questions.question_text: The residential property tax assessment rate for local government in Colorado (2025) is:
questions.explanation: For tax year 2025, Colorado uses a split assessment: 6.25% for local government and 7.05% for school districts. These rates are subject to legislative adjustment.

questions.question_text: Commercial property in Colorado is assessed at what percentage of actual value?
questions.explanation: Commercial property is assessed at 27% of actual value, significantly higher than the residential rate. This rate was reduced from the previous 29%.

questions.question_text: A qualifying senior (65+) who has lived in their Colorado home for 10+ years may receive:
distractors: A 25% reduction in the mill levy
A freeze on assessed value increases
questions.explanation: The Senior Homestead Exemption exempts 50% of the first $200,000 of actual value (up to $100,000 exemption) for qualifying seniors 65+ who have lived in the home 10+ years.

questions.question_text: Colorado property taxes are due in two installments. The second half is due by:
questions.explanation: First half is due by the last day of February. Second half is due June 15. Full payment can be made by April 30.

questions.question_text: Managing rental property for others in Colorado requires:
distractors: No license for fewer than 10 units
A separate PM license from DORA
questions.explanation: A real estate license is required to manage property for others for compensation in Colorado. There is no separate property management license.

questions.question_text: As of 2026, the maximum security deposit a Colorado landlord can require is:
questions.explanation: HB25-1249 (effective January 1, 2026) caps security deposits at one month's rent, down from no statutory maximum previously.

questions.question_text: After a tenant moves out, the Colorado landlord must return the security deposit within:
distractors: 60 days if specified in the lease
questions.explanation: Under HB25-1249, the default return period is 30 days after lease termination with an itemized statement. The lease may extend this to up to 60 days.

questions.question_text: Colorado's warranty of habitability for rental properties:
questions.correct_answer: Cannot be waived by the landlord or tenant
distractors: Can be waived in the lease agreement
Applies only to buildings over 20 years old
questions.explanation: The warranty of habitability was strengthened in 2008 and cannot be waived by either party. Landlords must maintain premises in habitable condition.

questions.question_text: A Colorado landlord must provide how many days' notice to terminate a month-to-month tenancy?
questions.explanation: Colorado requires 21-day notice for month-to-month tenancy termination. Nonpayment of rent requires 10-day notice, and substantial lease violations require 10-day notice.

questions.question_text: Colorado foreclosures are typically handled by the:
questions.explanation: Colorado is a deed of trust state. Foreclosures are typically non-judicial, handled by the public trustee. Judicial foreclosure is available but rarely used.

questions.question_text: The first step in a Colorado non-judicial foreclosure is filing a:
distractors: A lis pendens with the county
Notice of default with the assessor
questions.explanation: The lender files a Notice of Election and Demand (NED) with the Public Trustee, who then records it and notifies the borrower.

questions.question_text: In a Colorado foreclosure, the borrower can cure the default until:
questions.correct_answer: 12 noon the day before the sale
distractors: The moment the sale officially begins
30 days after the NED was filed
questions.explanation: Colorado provides a cure period until 12 noon on the day before the foreclosure sale. The borrower can stop the process by paying all past-due amounts.

questions.question_text: Since 2008, a Colorado borrower's right to stop a foreclosure ends when?
questions.correct_answer: At the foreclosure sale — no post-sale redemption
distractors: One year after the foreclosure sale
questions.explanation: Since 2008, Colorado borrowers no longer have post-sale redemption rights. Their only remedy is the pre-sale cure right (pay all arrears by noon the day before the sale). Junior lienholders retain limited post-sale redemption rights.

questions.question_text: After a Colorado foreclosure sale, if the proceeds are less than the debt, the lender may:
distractors: File a new deed of trust
questions.explanation: Colorado allows deficiency judgments. If the foreclosure sale proceeds are insufficient to cover the debt, the lender can pursue the borrower for the remaining balance.
